Footnotes

  1. Source: Bloomberg L.P., as of Apr. 11, 2025. The S&P 500 Index started at 5074.08 on Apr. 4, 2025, fell to 4982.77 on Apr. 8, 2025, rose to 5456.9 on Apr. 9, 2025, and finished at 5636.36 on Apr. 11, 2025. The 10-year US Treasury yield closed at 4.009% on Apr. 4, 2025, and closed at 4.494% on Apr. 11, 2025. Gold rose from 3038.24 on Apr. 4, 2025, to 3237.61 on Apr. 11, 2025.

  2. Source: University of Michigan Survey of Consumers, preliminary April survey, Apr. 11, 2025.

  3. Source: New York Fed Survey of Consumer Expectations, Apr. 14, 2025.

  4. Source: Chief Executive poll, Apr. 14, 2025.

  5. Source: NFIB Small Business Optimism Index, Apr. 8, 2025.

  6. Source: University of Michigan Survey of Consumers, preliminary April survey, Apr. 11, 2025.

  7. Source: Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget, May 10, 2024.

Important information

NA4408242

All investing involves risk, including the risk of loss.

Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Investments cannot be made directly in an index.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

Investments in companies located or operating in Greater China are subject to the following risks: nationalization, expropriation, or confiscation of property, difficulty in obtaining and/or enforcing judgments, alteration or discontinuation of economic reforms, military conflicts, and China’s dependency on the economies of other Asian countries, many of which are developing countries.

The Consumer Price Index (CPI) measures the change in consumer prices and is a commonly cited measure of inflation.

Fluctuations in the price of gold and precious metals may affect the profitability of companies in the gold and precious metals sector. Changes in the political or economic conditions of countries where companies in the gold and precious metals sector are located may have a direct effect on the price of gold and precious metals.

Gross domestic product (GDP) is a broad indicator of a region’s economic activity, measuring the monetary value of all the finished goods and services produced in that region over a specified period of time.

Hard data measures specific figures such as hiring or wages (while soft data measures indicators such as consumer sentiment and other economic opinions).

Inflation is the rate at which the general price level for goods and services is increasing.

NFIB Small Business Optimism Index is a composite of ten seasonally adjusted components calculated based on the answers of around 620 NFIB members.

The S&P 500® Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of the US stock market.

Soft data measure indicators such as consumer sentiment and other economic opinions (while hard data measure-specific figures such as hiring or wages).

Stagflation is an economic condition marked by a combination of slow economic growth and rising prices.

In general, stock values fluctuate, sometimes widely, in response to activities specific to the company as well as general market, economic and political conditions.

The New York Fed’s Survey of Consumer Expectations is a nationally representative, internet-based survey of a rotating panel of approximately 1,300 household heads.

The Survey of Consumers is a monthly telephone survey conducted by the University of Michigan that provides indexes of consumer sentiment and inflation expectations.

The opinions referenced above are those of the author as of Apr. 14, 2025. These comments should not be construed as recommendations, but as an illustration of broader themes. Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future results. They involve risks, uncertainties and assumptions; there can be no assurance that actual results will not differ materially from expectations.

Treasuries, sentiment, and earnings: What investors need to watch by Invesco US

data.title: What FAANG Should Mean In 2025 - My 5 Bullish Picks In A Tariff-Shaken Market
data.description:
It's been over a decade since the acronym FANG was introduced. It originally represented the high-growth tech leaders of the 2010s, Facebook (META), Amazon (AMZN), Netflix (NFLX), and Google (GOOGL), (GOOG). It evolved over the years, expanding into FAANG, where the additional 'A' stood for Apple (AAPL). The last version was FAAMG, where Microsoft (MSFT) replaced Netflix. At some point, the modified acronym wasn't cool anymore, and The Magnificent Seven nickname took its place. The media-friendly label included the FAAMG stocks together with NVIDIA (NVDA) and Tesla (TSLA). These have been the darlings of Wall Street, accounting for 32.0% of the S&P 500's (SPY) value index as of March 24, 2025.

The most recent market selloff created an excellent buying opportunity for long-term investors. To me, it was also the birth of a FAANG 2.0 pack. All of them offer solid growth, possess strong fundamentals, have moats and outstanding management teams, are global leaders in their industries, and are great businesses being either fairly- or undervalued. Buying them now will likely give investors ample results and peace of mind.

Facebook (Currently Meta)

One of the two original FANG names in FAANG 2.0 is Meta Platforms, formerly known as Facebook. This founder-led social media pioneer keeps growing despite its size, innovating and rewarding its shareholders.

Moat

Meta's biggest competitive advantage is the daily engagement of a staggering 3.35 billion users with its products and services.

Excluding China, where Meta and its offerings are banned, the company serves 49.34% of the global population every day. The expectation that the number of daily active users (DAU), or now, the Family Daily Active People (DAP), would stagnate or decrease has never come to fruition. Besides successfully running its Family of Apps, Meta focuses on much more complex projects within its Reality Labs segment.

This includes exploring new technologies such as neural interfaces using electromyography, which lets people control their devices using neuromuscular signals, as well as innovations in AI and hardware to help build next-generation interfaces. In the near term, we are continuing to develop early metaverse and wearables products and experiences that help people feel connected, anytime, anywhere.

Innovation and growth at Meta seem like safe bets. They should drive sales and revenue increases, and market capitalization will likely follow.

Valuation

With the expected EPS growth of 16.99% CAGR in the next 3 to 5 years, Meta's Price to Earnings and Price to Free Cash Flow look reasonable.

The multiples are on the lower end of the 5-year period, excluding the time when Meta suffered a strong stock selloff in 2022 and 2023. This was due to the margin and EPS contraction caused by the high capital expenditures related to Metaverse.

Profitability

Meta is a textbook example of a well-run business when it comes to profitability. The company's profitability grade on Seeking Alpha is an astonishing A+.

Meta shareholders can be delighted with a steady gross margin for over a decade and operating and profit margins in the range of 20% to 50%. Currently, the company achieves an excellent 37.91% profit margin mark.

The three other very important profitability metrics, Return on Assets (ROA), Return on Equity (ROE), and Return on Invested Capital (ROIC), are reaching new highs. With the ROIC at 38.65%, Meta is a rare example of a company where over a third of the money it uses to run its business comes back in the form of a Net Operating Profit After Tax (NOPAT). Meta is a Buy.

Adobe

Adobe (ADBE) is a great, asset-light business with a strong brand. The company commits to focusing on customers who can expect innovative products throughout the next decade.

Moat

Over the years, Adobe has transformed into a recurring revenue beast offering creative software to its loyal customers. Just between 2021 and 2024, its segments generated recurring / subscription revenue at a double-digit compound annual growth rate (CAGR).

  • Document Cloud - Average Recurring Revenue growth of 23.0% CAGR from $1.9 billion to $3.5 billion,
  • Creative Cloud - Average Recurring Revenue growth of 12.0% CAGR from $10.3 billion to $13.9 billion,
  • Experience Cloud - Subscription Revenue growth of 13.0% from $3.4 billion to $4.9 billion.

If these growth rates continue, sales should double every five to six years. With share buybacks, as the company currently does, EPS will grow even faster. Assuming the company is fairly valued now, the stock price should double in five years. That would be a 100% return on investment.

Adobe's products are the industry standard, and AI will only boost the business. What will also drive growth is the expanding customer base.

Valuation

Adobe's stock price has been falling for a while, just to accelerate downwards after the announcement of the reciprocal tariffs on the 2nd of April. Yet, the company is expected to grow its EPS by 14.9% CAGR for the next 3 to 5 years. Assuming the expected growth materializes and halves in the subsequent five years, the fair value per share is at the level of the current stock price.

Worth noting is the terminal multiple set to 15.0, corresponding with the historical average market P/E ratio. If Adobe is a great business, with innovation and growth over the next decade, and keeps its moat, this number should be higher. However, with these conservative assumptions, Adobe remains an excellent business trading for a fair value.

Profitability

The company's asset-light, software-as-a-service (SaaS) business model gives it an advantage in the form of high margins.

The gross margin has been trending up for years, reaching almost 90.0%. The operating and profit margins have been above 30.0% for the last seven years. That's a great position to be in the case of an economic slowdown or a recession. Solid margins let the management and the shareholders sleep well at night.

Return on Equity (ROE), which is the return on the capital owned by the shareholders, has reached 46.9%. This shows that the management allocates money exceptionally efficiently. The business is capable of returning the capital invested in it in almost two years! Adobe is a Buy.

Alibaba

The Chinese e-commerce giant can't catch a breath between heavy punches from the local government and the West. Just when the future started looking bright, tariffs appeared. Regardless of the global shakeup, Alibaba (BABA) remains one of the most innovative businesses in the world.

Moat

The business generates more than two-thirds of its revenue from its e-commerce platforms. Alibaba has constantly expanded into new areas such as logistics, digital media, and entertainment. Currently, Alibaba is a top player in the AI space and in cloud computing. The company also develops its own chips. Its strategy to diversify into various fields and take on new projects is the company's strength. Its Cainiao Smart Logistics Network and Cloud Intelligence Group are profitable on an EBITDA basis and keep growing. Alibaba also diversifies geographically through its retail and wholesale businesses, but not only. The company's cloud unit just recently upgraded its AI services for its customers abroad. Its large language model, the reasoning model, or the visual reasoning model are getting traction and will likely contribute significantly to the business growth.

Valuation

In my most recent article, Alibaba: A Great Business Doesn't Have To Be A Great Investment, I came up with an intrinsic value of $154.33 per share. The discounted earnings model, which the valuation was based on, accounted for an EPS growth of 19.1% CAGR in the next five years and 9.6% CAGR in the subsequent five years. The discount rate of 10.0% and a terminal multiple of 15 were assigned.

Alibaba also looks reasonable based on its Price to Free Cash Flow multiple, which is below the historical average.

Another factor that drives the EPS higher is share buybacks. Alibaba launched an aggressive repurchase program, repurchasing $43.13 billion worth of shares over a four-year period.

If Alibaba keeps growing its diversified portfolio of businesses and continues stock buybacks, a significant upside may be expected.

Profitability

Gross and operating margins experienced contraction since 2015. However, they stabilized and started slowly growing again. An operating margin above 10.0% is still decent.

The company's investments and bets in different areas may justify the margin contraction. It also needs time before its successful segments become profitable.

The chart showing Alibaba's ROA, ROE, and ROIC paints a similar profitability picture. From the inflection point in 2022, they all started trending up. Today's values aren't excellent but promising. Yet, considering the low valuation and growth ahead, Alibaba is a Buy.

Nike

NIKE (NKE), a beaten-down apparel titan, has been suffering for too long, and tariffs were its nail in the coffin. The stock price is down over 30.0% in a month and has experienced tremendous volatility in recent days. Yet, the brand is strong, the fundamentals are concrete, and the sun will shine again over the Oregon-based giant.

Moat

An undeniable competitive advantage Nike has is its brand and its recognition worldwide.

According to the ranking of the 50 apparel brands, Nike is second and the only American in the top 10, with a monetary (intangible) value estimated at $29.87 billion.

Michael Jordan, Kobe Bryant, and LeBron James embody the last four decades of basketball, and all of them wore Nike.

Cristiano Ronaldo stands for greatness in soccer, and Kylian Mbappe and Erling Haaland may well be his successors. All of them are Nike athletes.

Besides that, Nike's swoosh is, according to many rankings, the most recognizable logo worldwide.

Having brand-new Nike shoes is still a desire for thousands of teenagers. The Jumpman trademark is still alive and well, although Michael Jordan left the Chicago Bulls over twenty-five years ago. Athletes are always role models for many children. If Nike can sign them, the products will sell, even for exuberant prices.

Valuation

As Nike's stock price kept falling and reaching new lows, I kept assessing the company and getting ready to pull the trigger. In August 2024, Seeking Alpha was predicting a 6.7% EPS growth CAGR over the next 3 -5 years. Assuming it'll materialize over the next five years and in the subsequent five-year period, growth halves, the company would earn $4.84 per share in 2034. Assigning a 10.0% discount rate and a terminal P/E ratio of 15, the fair value is $52.14 a share.

If assumptions line up with future developments, Nike will return roughly 10.0% CAGR over the next ten years.

Profitability

Nike is a business with stable margins over periods of time.

Operating and profit margins in the range of 10.0% are healthy for an apparel business. Tariffs may cause a dent, but they may also lead to a shift in Nike's manufacturing strategy. This may force the company to come up with solutions to reduce expenses related to labor and the production process.

Nike has a solid ROIC, which shows that the capital invested in the business produces high returns.

Return on equity is above 30.0%, reaching highs above 45.0% not long ago. This is another sign of a great business. If the profitability ratios remain in the ranges from the last ten or more years, Nike's shareholders shouldn't worry about their investment. Nike is a Buy.

Google (Alphabet)

Google is the second from the original FANG and the last in FAANG 2.0. The company has been at the forefront of innovation for years and is not slowing down.

Moat

Google and Meta control more than half of the global digital advertising market, with Google having the largest market share, around 40.0%. The word Google became a verb that confirms the adoption and awareness of the service achieved. It's hard to imagine a day in life without using Google products such as YouTube, Android devices, the search engine, or Chrome. Google is also deeply involved in the AI revolution. The company develops its large language models and embedded AI tools in its browser and other services. A moonshot project Google is working on is quantum computers, which will be able to solve problems exponentially faster than the machines used currently. Above all, Google generates a ton of cash each year, with $72.76 billion in FY2024, giving it a lot of room to play.

Valuation

After the recent sell-off, Google trades at a forward P/E ratio of 17.14, compared to the average of 25.25 over the last five years.

The forward P/E ratio is at the lowest level in a decade, except for a correction period in the Fall of 2022. Price to Free Cash Flow dropped significantly and is trending downwards.

Besides the acquisitions and investments Google makes, the company has reduced its share count by roughly 10.0% over the last decade. This means that investors' share in the company has grown by 10.0% without doing anything. One can argue that buybacks make sense only when a company Is undervalued. Google has been buying back its shares almost the whole time, disregarding the valuation. However, the result for the shareholders is a 10.0% increase in their ownership of Google.

Profitability

Google is another asset-light profitability king.

With a stable gross profit margin of 58.2% and a profit margin of 28.6%, Google doesn't have to fear a recession. The business has an excellent track record, where the only time of distress was during the pandemic. Such margins are evidence that the business is well run. A squeeze in profits won't result in damage, and the profitability metrics prove that the company has enough buffer in difficult times.

Besides margins, Google has had a fantastic ten-year period of rising returns on assets, equity, and invested capital. This only confirms that the quality of the business has improved and that the management is doing a great job. Google is a Buy.

Conclusion

The FAANG 2.0 includes Facebook, Adobe, Alibaba, Nike, and Google—five wonderful, undervalued (or fairly valued) businesses to own for the next decade. Like any company, they all have risks. However, the possible reward from an investment in them outweighs these risks.

Facebook and Google might be prone to cyberattacks, or to a disruption from AI. There might also be regulatory struggles or high fines imposed by certain countries, as it already happened in the past.

Adobe's business might also be disrupted by AI. In addition, competitors may offer cheaper services that fulfill the same customers' needs. In times of crisis, advertising, marketing, and entertainment may suffer more than other areas.

Alibaba is a Chinese giant that has faced scrutiny from its own government. Due to the ongoing tensions between China and the West, it may be rejected internationally. The business may be outpaced by its rivals since Alibaba operates in a dynamic environment.

The only FAANG 2.0 name offering solely physical products is Nike. The variety of brands and changing customer tastes may disrupt the business. Tariffs pose a danger to Nike's margins, and its product prices may rise significantly.

These are the known risks the companies face. There are also unknown risks that nobody can predict. However, with stock prices significantly lower than before the tariff announcements and with great underlying businesses, the companies offer ample returns for investors. FAANG 2.0 is a Buy.

data.title: GLD: Driven By The Dollar, Interest Rates, The Deficit - And Above All, Fear
data.description:
Tariffs yes, tariffs no, tariffs maybe, it’s starting to feel like a lottery. While some wonder whether Trump’s policies are driven by hidden agendas, and others believe it's just the result of an unconventional mindset, the financial markets are moving billions in value each day amid mounting uncertainty. Like it or not, the only asset class maintaining a steady upward trend is gold, particularly the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:GLD).

As a financial researcher working closely with investment banks and independent firms, I can say there's now more debate around gold than ever. Should portfolios start making significant allocations to real assets like gold? Or is the risk simply jumping on a moving train that’s nearing its final stop?

Spoiler alert: I believe the outlook remains positive, but there’s a growing inconsistency that must be closely watched. Here’s what I mean.

Why GLD

Specifically, one of the most widely recognized references for gaining exposure to gold is GLD, with an AUM of $95.96B. It's one of the ETFs at the center of record inflows in recent weeks, $8.6 billion in net inflows into gold ETFs in March alone, equivalent to 92 tonnes of the precious metal, marking a record-setting quarter.

This highlights two key points:

  • There's strong demand for ETFs as the preferred vehicle when investors decide to add gold to their portfolios.

  • There’s also strong interest even at these price levels, despite gold trading near all-time highs.

Holding GLD in a portfolio comes with a 0.40% annual expense ratio, paid to SPDR, which in return holds physical gold bars on your behalf, something it’s been doing since 2004, making it one of the most established gold ETFs on the market.

A (Theoretically) Still-Positive Outlook for GLD

Do you also get that feeling? That sense of an irrational market, cluttered with media noise and distracting background chatter, making it hard to truly hear what prices are trying to tell us. If we try to remove all that noise, how do we actually analyze GLD’s performance? In theory, through real rates and the US dollar. And right now, those are the two factors that, regardless of the analysis perspective, everyone seems to want lower.

  • If interest rates fall, refinancing, both public and private, becomes less burdensome.

  • If the dollar weakens, tariffs might not even be needed to rebalance trade.

That’s the core narrative I’d like to focus on.

So I ask: What is the market doing right now?

  • The U.S. dollar has indeed pulled back.

  • Meanwhile, the bond market is still held hostage by political noise: The MOVE Index continues to rise. The VIX is holding above 35, which is not all that common. And the political uncertainty index is above the COVID-19 peak levels.

These are 2 clear signs that, despite last Thursday’s rally, the delay on tariffs, and encouraging economic data, like CPI falling to 2.4% and initial jobless claims stable at 223,000, markets are still navigating a sea of “trust deficit.”

So to summarize:

  • On rates: High uncertainty

  • On the dollar: Clear downward trend

Now I ask myself: How has gold, and by extension, the GLD ETF, historically performed in times of uncertainty and a weakening dollar?

3 historical events supporting a positive outlook

I like to highlight 3 historical events in which political uncertainty was high while the U.S. dollar experienced a significant decline:

  • The post-Bretton Woods era, 1971-1978, when the dollar will fall 35% from its pre-Nixon peak. A time of high inflation, and uncertainty. Gold was in an upward trend.

  • 1985–1988: The Plaza Accord led to coordinated intervention by major economic powers to devalue a dollar that was then considered too strong. Gold confirmed an upward trend (along with equity markets).

  • 2002- 2008, in the post “dot-com bubble”, period and amid high geopolitical tensions related to the Iraq war (gold rallied from previous lows).

Interestingly, and somewhat off-topic, among these three market phases, only in 1985–1988 did the equity market record gains, likely because the dollar’s decline was framed as a strategic move to improve the U.S. economy.

Outlook for GLD

Continuing with the logic of dollar trends, interest rates, and uncertainty, I note the following:

  • Real interest rates = US10Y - US CPI; still at cycle highs, with negative outlook.
  • DXY is declining, with negative prospects for the dollar amid trade war developments.
  • VIX remains above 35, signaling high uncertainty.

So overall, there appears to be a statistically favorable environment for gold prices to rise, despite the fact that gold is already trading near its all-time highs.

A potential game-changer (risk)

I'd like to discuss a fourth factor: the U.S. public deficit. Historically, gold has shown a strong correlation with the burden of the U.S. public deficit, which, as you may know, is currently quite pronounced.

In this context, the deficit supports demand for GLD. However, I believe it also creates a strong inconsistency with the narrative outlined in the earlier paragraphs.

What do I mean? In the first half of FY 2025, over 40% of the total deficit is attributed to the weight of interest payments on debt, due to rates still at decade highs. Out of more than $1.3 trillion in deficits, more than $500 billion was spent on interest alone. So, a significant portion of the increase is due to higher rates, rather than “reckless policies.”

As a result, one school of thought argues that gold should rise because expectations for real Treasury yields are falling. But this contradicts the idea that the market is buying gold due to a widening deficit, a deficit largely caused by high interest rates, not increased spending.

This logical inconsistency between the two main narratives supporting gold’s rise represents, in my opinion, a risk factor (or at least an area of uncertainty) for investors.

This is also evident in the fact that, despite geopolitical turmoil, a declining dollar, and the VIX exceeding 35, when the market initially reacted to the tariffs announced in early April with a sharp drop in Treasury yields and rising expectations of rate cuts in 2025 (to as low as 300–325 bps, according to the CME FedWatch Tool), gold began to lose value. It only returned to its highs once those expectations deflated, or rather, reversed, with yields rising again, and the new target rate priced at 350–375 bps (with a 32% probability).

This highlights a clear discrepancy between economic theory and market behavior in today’s environment.

A broader perspective

I’ve always maintained a strongly positive outlook on precious metals, and I believe the current market environment continues to offer fertile ground, especially for gold. However, I’m starting to notice a significant inconsistency between the theoretical narrative and the actual behavior the market is beginning to adopt.

For this reason, I prefer to give GLD a HOLD rating, rather than aligning with the widely shared LONG view.

data.title: Have We Hit Peak Pessimism In The U.S. Stock Market?
data.description:
A relative stability of the S&P 500 index over the last several days looks like a much-needed breather after weeks of non-stop volatility. However, as I've already mentioned in my previous article, this breather may be very well short-lived as the current macro setup doesn't favor further market recovery.

Nonetheless, the latest data from sentiment indicators shows that a reversal in the US market may be swift if the Trump administration manages to de-escalate the ongoing trade war fast enough. Let's take a look at a variety of sentiment indicators to have a clearer picture of the current market sentiment.

What Investors Say (And Do)

In its most recent survey, Bank of America (BAC) polled 164 investors with $386 billion of assets under management. According to the survey, the current level of investor sentiment is 5th lowest on record, providing us with a first data point that indicates a potential for a market reversal.

At the same time, recession expectations rose to the highest level since November 2022. Although, we should notice that on a relative scale, only 42% of investors expect a recession at this point, which is substantially lower compared to 80% expecting a recession in November 2022 and April 2020. Thus, in terms of recession fears, we aren't even close to peak pessimism.

Another interesting point is that a record amount of global investors intend to cut exposure to US equities. If these intentions translate into real selling of the US equities, then the US stocks could face a potentially significant selling overhang.

According to Bloomberg, foreigners own over $18 trillion out of roughly $52 trillion of US stocks, or around 34% of total equity ownership. In this regard, the risk of increased selling by foreigners shouldn't be underestimated.

On the bullish side, the GS US Equity Sentiment Indicator continues to demonstrate a deeply negative sentiment in US stocks, which means a decent potential for active buying of US equities back if the macro situation improves in the foreseeable future.

Also, an equity positioning indicator from Deutsche Bank shows a similar deeply negative dynamic, hinting that positioning in the US equities has bottomed, at least in the short term.

Moreover, an insider transactions ratio is going deeper into the bullish zone, indicating that insiders are selling not so actively compared to buying.

According to the indicator, the ratio of sales to buys under 12:1 is considered to be bullish, and the current ratio of 6 is well below this "bullish" threshold. In my opinion, insider actions are one of the more reliable sentiment indicators that is closely tied to the economic situation. When business owners and top managers aren't sure about the future, they will be likely to be the first ones to reflect this sentiment in the form of insider actions.

To finish with sentiment indicators, the last one I'd like to share is the so-called "Euphoriameter", reflecting a combination of forward P/E, VIX, and bullish sentiment numbers.

Based on this indicator, we haven't hit peak pessimism in the US market yet, and basically, we're only halfway to the point of a real market panic we've seen in 2009, 2020, or 2022.

Why It Matters?

Sentiment matters because the deeper sentiment indicators go into a "bullish" area, the faster market recovery we can expect once fundamentals improve. In this regard, a critical consideration to a possible recovery in the US stock market is whether the US economy is about to enter a recession. Investment bankers have revised their forecasts after the announcement of a 90-day tariff reduction. For example, Goldman Sachs lowered the probability of a recession in the US to 45% from 65% earlier.

To make it clear, I'm following forecasts of investment banks not because they're right (from my experience, they're too often not), but because they influence the sentiment and positioning of larger investors that rely on their research.

Even though I don't believe in miracles (an immediate mutual lifting of all tariffs, for example), I'm not in a "recession is inevitable" camp, especially after continuously listening to all the fearful comments about an upcoming recession since 2022.

The Bottom Line

Overall, sentiment indicators show mixed signals. Some of them demonstrate a decent potential for a rapid market recovery, and some show that there's plenty of room for more bearish market positioning.

In fact, we are now at an important crossroads. If the Trump administration somehow manages to strike trade deals, let's say, under a month or two with a mutual reduction of tariffs, then the risk of recession will likely decline, and the market recovery will not take long to come, at least based on sentiment indicators.

However, if all what we've seen so far is just a prelude to a long and exhausting trade war coupled with a painful recession in the US, then sentiment indicators should go much deeper to call a bottom in the market. On top of that, the existing negative sentiment of global investors may exacerbate the selling in the US market in case of prolonged trade tensions.

For risk-averse investors, I wouldn't recommend actively buying US stocks at the moment before we have more clarity on tariffs, though I don't see reasons to be excessively bearish as well, at least for now.
